solution.p: We will factor y^{2}+11y+24 where a=1, b=11, and c=24.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For y^{2}+11y+24, since the product of 8 and 3 is 24, and the sum of 8 and 3 is 11, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+11y+24\amp =\left(y+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(y+3\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (24) = 24.

The number 24 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(24)\amp -1(-24)\amp 2(12)\amp -2(-12)
ewline3(8)\amp -3(-8)\amp 4(6)\amp -4(-6)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 11, and this sum happens with 3+8=11 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+11y+24\amp =y^{2}+3y+8y+24
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y+3ight)+8\mathopen{}\left(y+3ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y+8ight)\mathopen{}\left(y+3ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}+11y+24 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term, 24, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(24)\amp -1(-24)\amp 2(12)\amp -2(-12)\newline3(8)\amp -3(-8)\amp 4(6)\amp -4(-6)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}+11y+24 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+11y+24\amp =\left(y+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(y+3\right)\end{aligned}
